title: Amos 6
subtitle: Woe to the Complacent in Zion

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

sections.header: Verses 1-7 — Luxury on the Eve of Exile

heb.paragraph: The term denotes not peaceful contentment but smug complacency — a false sense of security that insulates the elite from the reality of coming judgment. The word carries overtones of arrogance and willful ignorance.

The marzeaḥ was a well-attested institution in the ancient Near East: an elite banqueting club associated with excessive eating, drinking, and funerary rites. Ugaritic texts describe the marzeaḥ as a luxury dining society. Amos declares that this revelry will 'end' — the first to feast will be the first to go into exile.

refs.note: The parable of the rich man and Lazarus — a NT narrative exploring the same theme of luxury oblivious to suffering.
James' denunciation of wealthy oppressors echoes Amos' vocabulary and ethical demands.

notes.note: The woe is addressed to those 'at ease' in both Zion and Samaria. Complacency is a spiritual disease: it blinds people to their true condition before God.

The catalogue of luxury is devastating: ivory beds, choice meat, idle music (David's harp reduced to party entertainment), bowlfuls of wine, and expensive lotions. But 'you do not grieve over the ruin of Joseph.' Self-indulgence without compassion is condemned throughout Scripture.
'Therefore you will be among the first to go into exile, and your feasting and lounging will end.' The punishment precisely mirrors the crime: those first in privilege will be first in suffering.

Calvin draws a direct line from luxury to moral blindness: excessive comfort produces insensitivity to others' suffering. The elite of Samaria lived in a bubble of wealth while their nation was rotting from within.

The phrase 'improvise on musical instruments like David' may be sarcastic: the elite fancy themselves artists like David, but their music is merely entertainment, not worship. David composed psalms; they compose drinking songs.

The marzeaḥ (v. 7, translated 'revelry') was a known institution in the ancient Near East, attested in Ugaritic, Phoenician, and Aramaic texts as an elite banqueting society often associated with funerary rites.

Stuart reads the woe oracle as a sociological portrait of the ruling class. The inventory of luxury items is drawn from archaeological evidence of 8th-century Samaria: ivory inlays, imported foods, anointing oils. Stuart emphasizes that the fundamental charge is not wealth per se but the combination of wealth and indifference: 'they do not grieve over the ruin of Joseph' (v. 6). The marzeaḥ banquet (v. 7) is the institutional expression of this callous luxury.

Andersen and Freedman identify the luxury catalogue as a deliberate inversion of covenant blessing: the prosperity God gave Israel has been privatized by the elite and weaponized against the poor. The goods themselves are not evil, but their concentration in the hands of an indifferent ruling class constitutes systemic injustice.

hist.context: The 'woe' oracle targets the ruling class of both Judah ('those who are complacent in Zion') and Israel ('those who feel secure on Mount Samaria'). The detailed inventory of luxury — ivory beds, choice lambs, idle music, fine wine, premium lotions — is not merely descriptive but accusatory. These people 'do not grieve over the ruin of Joseph' (v. 6): their wealth has insulated them from the suffering of their own people. The punishment fits the crime: those who are first in luxury will be first in exile.

sections.header: Verses 8-14 — The LORD Swears by Himself

heb.paragraph: God swears by himself — the most solemn oath possible, since there is no greater authority by which to swear (cf. Heb 6:13). The object of his abhorrence is 'the pride of Jacob,' the arrogant self-sufficiency that has replaced dependence on God.

refs.note: 'Because God could swear by no one greater, he swore by himself' — the NT commentary on the solemnity of divine self-oath.
Jeroboam II restored Israel's borders from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah — the exact territory Amos says will be oppressed (v. 14).

notes.note: God swears by himself because there is no higher authority. He abhors Israel's pride and will deliver the city to destruction. When God takes an oath, the outcome is certain.
The scene of survivors hiding in houses, whispering 'Don't mention the name of the LORD,' depicts the terrifying aftermath of judgment. Even prayer seems dangerous when God's wrath is falling.

The rhetorical questions about horses running on rocks and plowing the sea illustrate the absurdity of Israel's behavior: they have turned justice into poison and the fruit of righteousness into bitterness.

Calvin emphasizes that a divine oath is the most irrevocable form of decree. When God swears by himself, no intercession, sacrifice, or repentance can alter the announced judgment. The time for repentance has passed.

The whispered 'Hush! We must not mention the name of the LORD' may reflect fear that invoking YHWH's name would draw further divine attention and judgment. The scene depicts a city so devastated that the survivors are paralyzed by terror.

The territory described ('from Lebo-hamath to the valley of the Arabah') corresponds precisely to the borders Jeroboam II restored (2 Kgs 14:25). The oppressing nation — unnamed but clearly Assyria — will control the very territory Israel recently recovered.

Stuart reads the death scene (vv. 9-10) as one of the most psychologically intense passages in prophetic literature. The whispering, the terror of naming God, the bodies in the house — all evoke the aftermath of siege and plague. The silence is the opposite of the noisy worship Amos condemned: now even God's name is too terrifying to speak.

Stuart notes the ironic reversal in v. 14: the very territory Jeroboam II reclaimed (a source of national pride, celebrated by Jonah's oracle in 2 Kgs 14:25) will be the territory oppressed by the coming enemy. Israel's greatest achievement becomes the measure of its loss.

Andersen and Freedman observe that God's self-oath (v. 8) parallels his oath 'by his holiness' in 4:2, creating a structural bracket around the central speeches. Both oaths guarantee judgment; together they form the theological backbone of divine certainty throughout the book.

hist.context: The section opens with God swearing by himself to deliver the city and everything in it. The scene in vv. 9-10 is haunting: a relative comes to carry out the dead and, finding a survivor hiding in the house, whispers 'Hush! We must not mention the name of the LORD.' The terror is so complete that even invoking God's name seems dangerous. The chapter closes with military futility (can horses run on rocks?) and the announcement of a nation that will oppress Israel 'from Lebo Hamath to the valley of the Arabah' — the full extent of Jeroboam II's restored territory.

themes.note: Judgment at 10: divine oath guarantees destruction. Justice at 9: the indictment of luxury and indifference is the chapter's driving force. Mercy at 1: this is the book's bleakest chapter, with no call to repentance.

rows.label: vv.1-7
rows.text: Woe Oracle: The Marzeaḥ Banquet and Exile

rows.label: vv.8-14
rows.text: Divine Oath and the Coming Oppressor

lit.note: Chapter 6 moves from social indictment (the complacent elite) to divine oath (judgment is certain) to military reversal (the territory will be lost). The structure mirrors chapters 1-2: accusation followed by punishment.

hebtext.note: The term denotes not peaceful contentment but smug complacency — a false sense of security that insulates the elite from the reality of coming judgment. The word carries overtones of arrogance and willful ignorance.

The marzeaḥ was a well-attested institution in the ancient Near East: an elite banqueting club associated with excessive eating, drinking, and funerary rites. Ugaritic texts describe the marzeaḥ as a luxury dining society. Amos declares that this revelry will 'end' — the first to feast will be the first to go into exile.

God swears by himself — the most solemn oath possible, since there is no greater authority by which to swear (cf. Heb 6:13). The object of his abhorrence is 'the pride of Jacob,' the arrogant self-sufficiency that has replaced dependence on God.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Woe to the Complacent in Zion

translations.text: You lie on beds adorned with ivory and lounge on your couches. You dine on choice lambs and fattened calves.
“Woe to those who lie on beds of ivory and stretch themselves out on their couches, and eat lambs from the flock and calves from the midst of the stall,
Therefore you will be among the first to go into exile; your feasting and lounging will end.
Therefore they shall now be the first of those who go into exile, and the revelry of those who stretch themselves out shall pass away.”

thread.text: Jeroboam II restored Israel's borders from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah — the exact territory Amos says will be oppressed (v. 14).

coaching.tip: 'Woe to those who are at ease in Zion' (v. 1). Complacency is condemned: lounging on ivory beds, improvising songs, drinking wine, anointing with oils — 'but are not grieved over the ruin of Joseph' (v. 6). Luxury anesthetizes. The wealthy have insulated themselves from national decay. Privilege becomes blindness.

'Therefore they shall now be the first of those who go into exile' (v. 7). Poetic justice: the first in luxury become the first in deportation. Those who reclined at the head of the table will lead the march to Assyria. Amos consistently shows punishment fitting crime. God's judgment is not random but precisely calibrated.
